introduction: Acute kidney injury is defined as a sudden clinical and/or laboratory manifestation of abnormal kidney function occurring within 48 hours or 7 days of kidney injury. A reduction in urine output documented as less than 0.5 ml/kg/hour for more than 6 hours. Absolute increase in serum creatinine of more than or equal to 0.3 mg/dl (26.5 μgmol/L) occurring within 48 hours of an insult to the kidneys. A percentage increase in serum creatinine of more than or equal to 50% (1.5 fold from baseline) which is known or presumed to have occurred within 7 days.

features: Gastrointestinal system: Nausea and vomiting, Hiccups, Diarrhoea, Epigastric pain and rarely haematemesis.
Chest: Central chest pain of pericarditis, Breathlessness, Pulmonary oedema.
Central nervous system: Tremors, Derangement of sleep rhythm, Drowsiness, Seizures, Stupor or coma.

non_drug: Maintenance of fluid homeostasis: Entails strict regulation of fluid intake to insensible loss (500 - 1200 ml/day).
Replacement of fluids totaling volume of urine and other documented losses in the previous 24 hours.
Maintenance of nutrition: High calorie low protein diet is recommended in acute or oliguric phase.
High calorie normal protein is recommended in recovery phase.
Parenteral hyperalimentation is seldom necessary in prolonged cases.
drug: Control of biochemical abnormalities: Hyperkalaemia treatment using Glucose-Insulin Infusion or Glucose Infusion, 10% Calcium gluconate, Dialysis or use of ion exchange resin like kayexelate.
supportive_measures: Dialysis where indicated. Types include: Daily haemodialysis, Extended daily dialysis, Slow low efficient dialysis, Acute peritoneal dialysis, Intermittent peritoneal dialysis, Haemofiltration or haemodiafiltration.

differential_diagnoses: Acute exacerbation of chronic kidney disease.
prevention: Avoidance of nephrotoxins in all forms.
Good maternal, child and reproductive health care
